topic: city hall rant — budget cuts to PFD

turns.content: I need to vent for a minute.

City council just released the proposed budget for next fiscal year. They're cutting the fire department again. Third year in a row. We're already running short on every shift and they want to cut more.
That's incredibly frustrating, especially when you're already stretched thin. What are they cutting specifically — personnel, equipment, station funding?

Overtime budget slashed. Two engine companies on the chopping block for brownouts. And they're delaying apparatus replacement again. We've got trucks running past their service life. It's a safety issue and they don't care.

Browning out engine companies is a dangerous game. Response times go up, coverage gaps appear, and the remaining companies get run harder. And running apparatus past service life isn't just a mechanical problem — it becomes a liability issue. Are the unions pushing back on this?

The union is fighting it. Local 22 put out a statement. But city hall doesn't listen to us until something goes wrong. Then it's a tragedy and everybody cares for a week. I've seen this play out three times in my career.

That's the pattern in a lot of cities — cuts happen quietly and the consequences don't show up until there's a crisis. By then the damage is done. Has the department leadership put together response time data showing the impact of previous cuts? That kind of hard data tends to get more traction with council members than statements alone.
We have the data. Response times are up 18% in the districts where they did brownouts last year. 18 percent. That's not a statistic. That's people waiting longer while their house is on fire.

An 18% increase in response time is significant and measurable. That's the kind of number that should be in front of every council member, in the press, and part of the public record at budget hearings. Is there a public comment period before the budget vote?
There's a hearing in two weeks. Some of the guys are going to testify. I'll probably go too. Not that it'll change anything but you have to show up. You have to put it on the record.

Showing up matters even when the outcome feels predetermined. It puts council members on notice that if something goes wrong, they were warned. And it gets media attention. If you testify, lead with the 18% response time number — concrete data is harder to dismiss than general concerns. Keep it short, factual, and let the numbers do the talking.
That's the plan. I'm not a speech guy. I'll give them the numbers, tell them what it looks like on the ground, and sit down. Two minutes.
Two minutes of hard facts from someone with decades on the job carries more weight than a twenty-minute speech. You'll make your point.
Alright. I feel better getting that off my chest. Thanks for listening.
Anytime. Good luck at the hearing. What you're fighting for matters.
